overview.body: Chapter 11 extends what you built in Chapter 10. If classes are blueprints, inheritance lets you build a new blueprint by extending an existing one. A Car is an Automobile with extra attributes. A Dog is a Mammal that makes a specific sound. Programs 11-1 and 11-2 build a vehicle hierarchy and are merged into one file. Program 11-3 demonstrates how to use those classes. Programs 11-9 and 11-10 demonstrate method overriding: the ability of a subclass to replace a superclass method with its own version. This is what makes polymorphism work.
objectives: Define a superclass with private attributes, a constructor, and getter/setter methods
Define subclasses using class SubClass(SuperClass): syntax
Call the superclass constructor from a subclass using SuperClass.__init__(self, args)
Override a superclass method in a subclass
Create objects from both superclasses and subclasses and call their methods
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 11 covers inheritance (Section 11.1), calling the superclass constructor (Section 11.1), and method overriding (Section 11.2). Programs 11-1 through 11-3 demonstrate a vehicle hierarchy. Programs 11-9 and 11-10 demonstrate overriding with animals.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis, Chapter 11
programRequirements: All four checkpoints are implemented and run without errors
Checkpoint 1 (vehicles.py) merges Programs 11-1 and 11-2 into a single class definition file
File has a top-of-file documentation header: Name, abc123, Section, Description
overview.filenameNote: Submit bookex-ch11.py with all four checkpoints combined into one file.

rows.task: Automobile superclass + Car, Truck, and SUV subclasses — merged into one file
Create Car objects and call inherited and subclass-specific methods
Mammal superclass + Dog and Cat subclasses with method overriding
Call make_sound() on objects of different types — same method, different behavior

content.body:
Programs 11-1 and 11-2 merged — Vehicle Hierarchy
These two Gaddis programs are combined into one file. Program 11-1 defines the Automobile superclass. Program 11-2 defines Car, Truck, and SUV as subclasses. This file contains class definitions only, with no main().

items: Define the Automobile superclass with an __init__ that accepts make, model, mileage, and price. Store all four in private attributes (self.__make, etc.). Write getter and setter methods for all four attributes.

Define the Car subclass: class Car(Automobile):. Its __init__ accepts all Automobile parameters plus doors. Call the superclass constructor: Automobile.__init__(self, make, model, mileage, price). Store self.__doors = doors. Add getter and setter for doors.
Define the Truck subclass the same way, but with a drive_type attribute (e.g., 'Two Wheel Drive') instead of doors.
Define the SUV subclass with a pass_cap (passenger capacity) attribute.

content.body: The subclass constructor calls Automobile.__init__(self, make, model, mileage, price) to initialize the inherited attributes. Without this call, the private attributes defined in Automobile.__init__ would never be created. The subclass would have no __make, __model, etc. Every subclass constructor must call the superclass constructor when the superclass uses __init__ to set up private attributes.

rows: Forgetting to call Automobile.__init__() in Car.__init__()
Inherited attributes never created. AttributeError when getters are called
Always call superclass __init__ first thing in the subclass constructor
Works in Python 3 but Gaddis uses the explicit form. Match the textbook
Use Automobile.__init__(self, make, model, mileage, price)
Redefining inherited getter/setter methods in the subclass
Redundant and creates confusion about which version runs
Only define what is new or overridden in the subclass

content.body:
Program 11-3 — Using the Vehicle Classes
This program imports vehicles and creates a Car object, demonstrating that inherited methods and subclass-specific methods both work through the same object.

items: Write main(). Get make, model, mileage, price, and number of doors from the user.
Create a Car object: car = vehicles.Car(make, model, mileage, price, doors).
Display the car's data by calling all five getter methods: the four inherited from Automobile and get_doors() from Car.

content.body: The Car object has access to both get_make() (inherited from Automobile) and get_doors() (defined in Car). You call both using the same dot notation — the inheritance is invisible to the caller. This is the design goal: a Car object behaves like an Automobile and can also do car-specific things.

Program 11-9 — Method Overriding
Define a Mammal superclass and two subclasses (Dog and Cat) that override the make_sound() method. This file contains class definitions only.

items: Define class Mammal:. Its __init__ accepts a species parameter and stores it in self.__species. Write show_species(self) that prints the species. Write make_sound(self) that prints 'Grrrrr'.
Define class Dog(Mammal):. Its __init__ takes no parameters other than self. Call Mammal.__init__(self, 'Dog'). Override make_sound(self) to print 'Woof! Woof!'.
Define class Cat(Mammal): the same way, with 'Cat' as the species and 'Meow' as the sound.
This file has no main() and no if __name__ guard. It is a class definition file.

content.body: Method overriding is the core of polymorphism. Both Dog and Cat inherit show_species() from Mammal and use it without change. But each provides its own make_sound(). When you call dog.make_sound(), Python looks at the Dog class first and finds the override. The superclass version is never reached. This means you can write one loop that calls make_sound() on a list of mixed animals and get the right sound for each one automatically.

Program 11-10 — Polymorphism in Action
This program imports animals and creates a list of animal objects. It then loops through the list calling make_sound() on each one, demonstrating that the same method call produces different behavior depending on the object type.

items: Write main(). Create a list containing one Mammal object, one Dog object, and one Cat object.
Write a for loop that iterates through the list. For each object, call show_species() and then make_sound().
Run it and observe: the same make_sound() call produces three different outputs depending on the object.

content.body: This is polymorphism: one interface, multiple behaviors. The loop does not know or care what type each animal is. It calls make_sound() and gets the right result. This is the same principle behind your game's City class in Module 5. When you call a method on a city object, the object itself knows what to do. You do not need a separate if/elif chain for each city type.

rows: Creating a Mammal object with animals.Mammal() with no arguments
TypeError: Mammal.__init__() requires a species argument
Pass a species string: animals.Mammal('Generic Mammal')
Dog handles the species internally: just use animals.Dog()
Expecting consistent output order from the list
List order is predictable. This is not a set issue
The order matches the order you added objects to the list

beforeYouSubmit: bookex-ch11.py contains all four checkpoints in order
Checkpoint 1 (vehicles.py) merges Programs 11-1 and 11-2: Automobile, Car, Truck, and SUV all in one class definition section
Checkpoint 3 (animals.py) is a class definition file only, with no main() inside it
Checkpoints 2 and 4 import their respective modules and include a main() with the __name__ guard
All four checkpoints run without errors
File has a top-of-file documentation header: Name, abc123, Section, Description
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapter 11 techniques: inheritance with class SubClass(SuperClass):, calling the superclass constructor with SuperClass.__init__(self, args), method overriding, and polymorphism through a list of mixed object types.

specific: Method overriding: redefining a superclass method in a subclass
isinstance() if needed for type checking
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: list comprehensions, multiple inheritance, abstract base classes

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read the step-by-step breakdown in your Chapter 11 BookEx Reference Guide before looking at the code
If you get AttributeError when calling an inherited getter, the superclass __init__ was probably not called in the subclass constructor
If Dog() or Cat() require an argument, check that you called Mammal.__init__(self, 'Dog'), not Mammal.__init__('Dog')
Submit to CodeGrade early to isolate which checkpoint is failing
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 11 BookEx Reference Guide, which includes annotated code showing the superclass constructor call pattern and method override syntax
